content:
Same type checker, new engine — native Go replaces JavaScript’s single-threaded ceiling.

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Here’s a moment every TypeScript developer knows by heart. You hit save on a big project, tsc spins up, and you wander off to make coffee. On VS Code’s own codebase, a full build used to take over two minutes.
Then TypeScript 7.0 landed, and that same build finished in about ten seconds.
Not a typo. Ten seconds.

The numbers that made everyone look twice
On July 9, 2026, Microsoft shipped TypeScript 7.0. The release blog led with a benchmark table that’s honestly a little hard to believe on first read.

VS Code is the headline act: 2.3 million lines of code, 125 seconds down to 10. If you run a full build ten times a day, that’s 21 minutes of waiting turned into under 2. Nineteen minutes back — enough to actually ship the feature you were compiling.
So where does an order-of-magnitude speedup come from? Not a smarter algorithm. Not a new data structure. Not some tuning flag nobody knew about.
Microsoft rewrote the entire compiler from TypeScript/JavaScript into Go.
That’s the whole story. Now let’s unpack why they had to.
The problem with compiling TypeScript in TypeScript
tsc has always been written in TypeScript. In language-nerd terms, it's self-hosting — the compiler compiles itself. GCC, Rust, Go, and Swift all do the same thing, and the payoff is dogfooding: design flaws surface the instant you go to build the compiler.
But self-hosting TypeScript means running the compiler on a JavaScript runtime (V8/Node), and that runtime comes with some hard ceilings:

• It’s single-threaded. Type-checking a.ts and b.ts is mostly independent work that should run in parallel. JavaScript can't do it well. Web Workers exist, sure — but they don't share memory. Every worker gets its own heap, and cross-thread talk means postMessage serialization. For a compiler that constantly reads a shared AST and symbol table, copying all that data across threads costs more than the parallelism buys you.

• GC pauses stall everything. A big project spawns millions of AST nodes. When V8’s garbage collector goes stop-the-world, the whole build freezes. Each pause is a few milliseconds — but stack them up across a 100-second build and it adds up.

• Watch mode polls. --watch is where you live all day. TS 6 polled the filesystem — checking every file's modified time on a timer. More files, more overhead.
None of this is a skill issue on the TypeScript team. It’s the JavaScript runtime’s ceiling. Keep the compiler on JS, and your performance is capped — full stop.
Why Go — and not Rust?
The second Microsoft said “Go,” the internet said “…wait, why not Rust?” Fair question. The frontend toolchain has been going full Rust for years now: SWC, Rolldown, Turbopack, Oxc. Rust is practically the reflexive answer to “make the JS tool fast.”
But look at what the TypeScript team actually needed: massive parallelism, fast iteration, good-enough performance, and a language the team could pick up quickly. Line those up and Go’s case makes itself.

• goroutines are featherweight. You can spin up thousands of concurrent parsing and checking tasks without breaking a sweat.

• Shared-memory parallelism is native. No wrestling a borrow checker to let two threads read the same AST.

• Go compiles blazingly fast — which matters enormously when you’re iterating on the compiler itself.

• The learning curve is gentle. The TS team are JS/TS experts, and Go is a far shorter hop than Rust’s ownership-and-lifetimes model.
Rust wins on raw single-threaded speed. But the TypeScript compiler’s bottleneck was never single-threaded speed — it was can we parallelize this efficiently. Go’s goroutines-plus-shared-memory model is the exact prescription.
One honest caveat: this is the community’s read, not an official design doc. Microsoft never published a detailed language-selection writeup, and the real decision surely involved team, timeline, and strategy factors I can’t verify. But on technical fit alone, Go is the sane pick.
The three things Go gives you that JavaScript can’t
1. Shared-memory parallelism. Goroutines read and write the same AST directly, syncing with a sync.Mutex or a channel. JS Workers can't — they copy. For type-checking, which hammers the global type table and symbol table nonstop, direct shared access is a different universe.
2. Native compilation. Go emits native machine code that runs straight on the CPU — no V8 JIT warmup, no interpreter tax. A compiler is a CPU-bound program (parse, infer, emit — all compute), and native code has a structural edge there.
3. Microsecond GC pauses. Go has a garbage collector too, but it’s concurrent and built for low latency. Pauses land in the microseconds versus V8’s milliseconds — effectively invisible during a build.
Where the speed actually comes from
The 10x isn’t one clever trick. It’s parallelism applied at every stage the old runtime used to block.
Parsing and emit — embarrassingly parallel. Turning source text into an AST, and ASTs back into .js/.d.ts files, is per-file work with no cross-file dependencies. TS 6 queued the files up one by one. TS 7 hands dozens of them to goroutines at once. This is exactly why the gains scale with codebase size — VS Code's 2.3M lines hit 11.9x, while small projects land around 7.7x.
Type checking — shared-memory parallel. This is the hard part, and the old bottleneck. Checking needs global information: where a type is defined, what an interface extends, what a generic is constrained to. You can’t naively shard it by file. TS 7’s move: multiple workers check different files at the same time, all reading one shared type table and symbol table — no copies, no message passing.

The logic itself didn’t change here — same type system, same results as TS 6. They rewrote the material, not the rules. (Third-party writeups mention a --checkers flag to tune the worker count; I couldn't find it in the official docs, so verify before you lean on it.)
The watch-mode plot twist. This is my favorite decision in the entire project. VS Code already had a great file watcher — @parcel/watcher, a C++ native module that taps the OS's own inotify (Linux) / FSEvents (macOS) / ReadDirectoryChangesW (Windows) APIs for instant change notifications, no polling. But the TS team didn't want a C++ toolchain dependency dragging along node-gyp and cross-platform native builds. No thanks.
So they ported Parcel’s watcher from C++ to Go. The whole thing, rewritten, keeping only a tiny assembly shim for one platform quirk. Parcel’s author, Devon Govett, got a shout-out in the 7.0 credits. It’s a full-circle ecosystem loop: a tool born to serve frontend bundlers now has its core running inside the TypeScript compiler. The result — --watch is instant. Change a file, the compiler knows immediately, no timer involved.
The upgrade — cheaper than you’d think
Install is boring, which is the whole point:
npm install -D typescript@7After that, tsc is the Go compiler. No extra config.
The catch is defaults. TS 7 inherits 6.0’s new defaults and promotes several deprecated options to hard errors. Jump straight from 5.x and the impact is real. Here’s the shortlist:

The two that bite hardest:

• types went from "auto-load every @types/*" to an empty list. Globals you used to get for free — node, jest, bun — now need to be declared explicitly:
{
"compilerOptions": {
"types": ["node", "jest"]
}
}
• target: "es5" is a hard error now. Gone completely, along with downlevelIteration. Bump to es2015 or higher.
The playbook is short: run npx tsc@7 --noEmit, see how many errors pop, fix the tsconfig.json bits, rebuild, and enjoy the clock. The config churn is a few lines. The speedup is the whole reason you're here.
What this actually changes day to day

• Your editor gets faster. Autocomplete, go-to-definition, error squiggles, find-all-references — all of it rides the TypeScript Language Server, and TS 7’s LSP is Go too, answering requests concurrently. That “waiting for TypeScript…” spinner on a big project shrinks hard.

• Your CI bill drops. Full builds going from minutes to seconds means less wall-clock and less compute — especially painful-to-watch on per-minute CI.

• Monorepos parallelize. Project references with no dependency between them build at the same time instead of standing in a queue.

• AI coding tools speed up too. Cursor, Copilot, and friends constantly query TypeScript for type context in the background. Faster type info means faster, better assists — easy to overlook, very real in practice.
The bigger signal
My first reaction: the frontend toolchain’s “just rewrite it in Rust” era might have sprung a leak.
For years, Rust was the reflexive answer to “make the JS tool fast” — SWC, Rolldown, Turbopack, Oxc, one after another. TypeScript picking Go says out loud that Rust isn’t the optimal answer for every problem.
The split is clean. Rust is for single-threaded, absolute-peak performance. Go is for large-scale parallelism plus fast iteration. The TypeScript compiler’s bottleneck was parallelism, not raw speed. Rust would’ve been faster in theory — maybe 12x instead of 10x — but Go hit the target with a shorter dev cycle and a lower learning cost. Why trade Rust’s theoretical best for Go’s engineering best?
And that’s the real lesson, which has nothing to do with compilers. You don’t pick the strongest tool. You pick the one that fits the job.
If this saved you a rabbit-hole through release notes, a clap 👏 (or fifty) helps other developers find it. And drop your TS 7 upgrade war stories in the comments — I read every one.
